description: The goblin shark is the sole living species of the family Mitsukurinidae and is one of the most distinctive-looking sharks on Earth. Its elongated, flattened snout is lined with electroreceptors (ampullae of Lorenzini) that detect prey in the lightless deep, and its jaw can project rapidly forward to engulf prey — a feeding strategy unique among living sharks. Soft-bodied and flabby, it is adapted for the low-energy environment of the deep sea and is rarely encountered by humans.
funFact: The goblin shark's jaws can project forward at up to 3.1 m/s, extending roughly 9% of its body length in a fraction of a second — one of the fastest jaw-protrusion events recorded in any shark, allowing it to snatch prey without needing to lunge its entire sluggish body.
habitat: Deep outer continental shelves, submarine canyons, and seamounts worldwide, typically at depths of 270–1,300 m. Most specimens have been caught in the Pacific (particularly around Japan) and the Atlantic, with occasional records from the Indian Ocean.
geographicRangeDescription: Has a circumglobal but sparse distribution in deep water across all major ocean basins. The largest number of confirmed specimens come from Japan (particularly off Tōkai and in Suruga Bay), with additional records from the Gulf of Mexico, the eastern Atlantic off Portugal and France, South Africa, Australia, and New Zealand. Most sightings are accidental bycatch from deepwater trawls and longlines, giving the species an air of mystery that belies its global range.
diet: Deep-sea teleost fish, squid, octopus, and crustaceans (particularly isopods and decapods). The highly protrusible jaws and prominent ampullae of Lorenzini suggest a sit-and-ambush strategy in near-total darkness, detecting the bioelectric fields of prey rather than hunting by sight.
size: Typically 3–4 m (9.8–13 ft) for adults; max confirmed ~6.2 m (20 ft). Females likely grow larger than males, as in many shark species. The body is notably soft and flabby compared to other lamniform sharks.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — deep-sea habitat makes population assessment extremely difficult; data deficient globally

vitalSigns.label: Lifespan
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — deep-sea longevity rarely documented; likely decades

vitalSigns.label: Top Speed
vitalSigns.value: ~5 km/h (~3 mph) estimated; slow cruiser adapted for deep-sea energy conservation

vitalSigns.label: Ecological Role
vitalSigns.value: Mesopredator / apex predator of the deep mesopelagic zone

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — no egg cases or nursery areas confirmed; presumed ovoviviparous with small litters

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Deep-sea fish, squid, octopus, and crustaceans; detected via bioelectric sensing in near-total darkness

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Highly protrusible slingshot jaws; elongated rostrum dense with ampullae of Lorenzini; translucent pink skin from superficial blood vessels; soft, low-density body for neutral buoyancy without an oil-filled liver

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: 270–1,300 m (885–4,265 ft); most specimens caught at 270–960 m

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Worldwide in temperate and tropical deep-water environments; confirmed in the Pacific (Japan, Australia, NZ), Atlantic (Gulf of Mexico, South Africa, France), and Indian Ocean

threats.name: Deepwater Trawl Bycatch
threats.description: Occasionally caught as bycatch in deepwater bottom trawls and longlines targeting commercially valuable species. Most recorded specimens entered science through accidental capture. The true rate of bycatch mortality is unknown but likely low given the very limited overlap between goblin shark depth ranges and targeted fisheries.

threats.name: Deep-Sea Mining
threats.description: Emerging commercial interest in polymetallic nodules and seafloor massive sulfide deposits on the outer shelf and slope directly overlaps with goblin shark habitat. The long-term disturbance of benthic prey communities and physical destruction of substrate pose an unquantified but real threat to this and all deep-sea species.

threats.name: Data Deficiency
threats.description: So little is known about goblin shark biology — reproduction, longevity, population size, or prey ecology — that effective management is essentially impossible. The IUCN Least Concern listing reflects lack of evidence of decline, not evidence of health. A significant population crash could go undetected for decades.

threats.name: Deep-Sea Pollution
threats.description: Persistent organic pollutants (PCBs, flame retardants, microplastics) accumulate in deep-sea food webs. Goblin sharks feeding on mesopelagic squid and fish are exposed to bioaccumulated contaminants. No direct studies have measured tissue burdens in this species.

actionItems.title: Support a Deep-Sea Mining Moratorium
actionItems.description: Deep-sea mining threatens some of the least-disturbed ecosystems on Earth, including goblin shark habitat. Organizations like the Deep Sea Conservation Coalition advocate for a moratorium on mining in international waters until comprehensive environmental impact assessments are complete.

actionItems.title: Fund Deep-Sea Research Programs
actionItems.description: The goblin shark is so poorly studied that basic biological facts remain unknown. Supporting institutions that deploy remotely operated vehicles (ROVs) and conduct deep-sea tagging studies directly contributes to the scientific foundation needed to protect this species.

actionItems.title: Advocate for Deepwater Trawl Restrictions
actionItems.description: Deepwater bottom trawling is ecologically destructive and is the primary way goblin sharks enter human hands. Urging fishing regulators to restrict trawling below 800 m — as the EU has done in some areas — protects both the shark and the fragile deep-sea ecosystems it depends on.

healthMetrics.label: IUCN Status
healthMetrics.value: Least Concern (2020) — but effectively data deficient; no evidence of decline is not evidence of stability

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Unknown — no population surveys have ever been conducted; trend inferred as stable by IUCN based on absence of data

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — deep-sea shelf and slope habitat largely intact but threatened by emerging deep-sea mining operations

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Largely unknown — deep mesopelagic prey base is understudied; indirect impacts from overfishing of squid and deep fish possible

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Low — no directed fishery exists; accidental bycatch in deepwater trawls at unknown but likely small scale

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Minimal — no species-specific protections exist; covered by general fisheries bycatch regulations in some jurisdictions

populationTrendMeta.note: No population surveys have ever been conducted for this species. Numbers are entirely speculative order-of-magnitude placeholders derived from rough habitat area estimates and the assumption of low but stable abundance — consistent with the IUCN Least Concern assessment. Actual population size is genuinely unknown.

preyDeclineRegions.name: NW Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Deep-sea squid and mesopelagic fish subjected to increasing commercial pressure; indirect impacts on goblin shark prey availability in the area where most specimens are recorded (around Japan)

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Deepwater squid and fish moderately fished; goblin shark encounters rare; prey dynamics poorly documented

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indian Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Minimal documentation of goblin shark occurrence; deep prey communities understudied

fishingPressureRegions.name: NW Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Occasional bycatch in Japanese deepwater trawl and longline fisheries; most formally documented specimens originate here; no directed fishery

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Rare bycatch in deepwater trawl operations; specimens occasionally recovered from the Gulf of Mexico and the Portuguese and French coasts

fishingPressureRegions.name: Southern Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Very limited documented interaction; some bycatch possible in Patagonian toothfish longline operations on deep seamounts
